planting.note: In zone 9, set dormant crowns or plugs October–November for January–March fruiting; Chandler is a short-day (June-bearing) strawberry that produces its primary crop when daylength is under 14 hours. The October–November planting in zone 9 triggers vegetative establishment before winter short days initiate flowering and fruit set.

directions: Plant dormant crowns with the crown at soil level in well-amended, raised beds or hill-and-furrow systems with good drainage; Chandler was released jointly by the California Strawberry Advisory Board and the USDA in 1983 and rapidly became the most widely planted commercial strawberry in the world. In California's Central Coast, it produces the majority of fresh market strawberries sold in the United States. In Florida and the Gulf Coast, it is the standard commercial season variety, planted October–November for harvest December–April. Chandler produces very large (1–2 oz), firm, bright red, conical berries with excellent sweet-acid flavor that remains consistent over an extended harvest season. The early-season production (December–January in zone 9) and large berry size made it the commercial benchmark. For home growers in zone 9, plant in raised beds with plastic mulch and drip irrigation; Chandler's vigor and large size reward careful management. Fruits can develop poorly in extreme heat — Chandler's season ends as temperatures rise above 80°F. Remove runners to keep plants productive. In zone 9, treat as an annual.
sources: USDA Agricultural Research Service — Chandler strawberry release notes 1983
University of California Cooperative Extension — Strawberry Variety Evaluation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Strawberry Production in Florida (HS-733)
California Strawberry Commission variety production data

planting.note: In zone 9, plant dormant crowns in October–November for harvest December–March; Sweet Charlie is an early-season variety that begins fruiting before Chandler, producing its first ripe berries in December in zone 9a–9b. Ideal for home gardens and farmers' markets where the earliest strawberries command premium prices.

directions: Plant dormant crowns with crown at soil level in well-drained, amended beds in full sun; Sweet Charlie is a short-day strawberry cultivar released by the University of Florida Institute of Food and Agricultural Sciences in 1992, bred specifically for warm climates and the production systems of the Gulf South and Florida. It consistently ranks as one of the earliest and sweetest commercial strawberries in Florida and the Gulf South — often the first variety to ripen by 2–3 weeks over Chandler, with exceptional flavor characterized by very high sugar content and intensely sweet berry aroma. The medium-sized fruits are softer than Chandler (limiting shelf life for commercial distribution) but deliver superior fresh-eating flavor — the classic choice for local farmers' markets and u-pick operations where fruit is eaten the same day. Sweet Charlie is popular with home gardeners who prefer flavor over shipping durability. It shows good tolerance of Florida's warm winters and performs well in zone 9 planted on the October–November schedule. Manage irrigation carefully — overwatering increases disease; underwatering reduces berry size.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Strawberry Breeding Program — Sweet Charlie release notes 1992
LSU AgCenter — Strawberry Variety Evaluation in Louisiana
Gulf Coast Regional Strawberry Growers Association production data
Plant City Strawberry Festival — Featured variety documentation

planting.note: Plant dormant crowns in October–November in zone 9 for a December–March crop; Camarosa is slightly later than Sweet Charlie but earlier than Chandler. It requires cool nights for best color and flavor development. Like all short-day strawberries in zone 9, treat as a winter annual planted each October.

directions: Plant dormant crowns with crown at soil level in raised, well-drained beds in full sun; Camarosa is a large-fruited, early-season strawberry developed by the University of California at Davis and released in 1992. For nearly a decade it was the most widely planted commercial strawberry in California and dominated early-season Florida production as well. The large, conical berries are dark red, very firm, and mildly sweet — excellent for shipping and processing but slightly less intensely flavored fresh than Sweet Charlie or Festival. Camarosa's principal strengths are its very high productivity, large and attractive berry appearance, and exceptional firmness that makes it the preferred variety for growers who need to ship or sell at farmers' markets where berries must look their best. In zone 9, Camarosa is planted October–November and harvested primarily in January–March. The plants are vigorous and productive but susceptible to powdery mildew and some soil-borne diseases — plant in raised beds with fresh soil or strawberry-specific fumigation. Maintain consistent drip irrigation to reduce disease and ensure uniform berry sizing.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Camarosa Strawberry Release Notes 1992
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Strawberry Variety Trials
California Strawberry Commission — Commercial variety statistics
LSU AgCenter — Strawberry Variety Evaluation for Louisiana

planting.note: Plant dormant crowns October–November in zone 9 for harvest December–April; Festival is a mid-to-late season short-day variety that extends the Gulf Coast strawberry harvest past the early-season varieties. It is the recommended variety for Florida's commercial strawberry production in the Plant City/Hillsborough County region.

directions: Plant dormant crowns or plugs with crown at soil level in well-drained, raised beds or hills in full sun; Festival is a mid-to-late-season short-day strawberry released cooperatively by the USDA and University of Florida in 2000 that has become the dominant commercial strawberry variety in Florida's Hillsborough County, the largest strawberry-producing county east of the Mississippi River. Festival was developed to balance production timing with Chandler and extend the Gulf Coast season; it begins producing 2–3 weeks after Sweet Charlie and Chandler and continues fruiting longer into the spring. Berries are medium to large, very attractive, firm, bright red, with good sweet flavor and an excellent fresh appearance that makes them the standard for appearance at retail and export markets. Festival's disease resistance to anthracnose (Colletotrichum acutatum) is significantly better than Chandler, making it more reliable in warm, humid conditions where anthracnose crown rot can devastate the crop. An excellent choice for Gulf Coast home gardens and commercial growers where the extended season and disease tolerance are priorities.
sources: USDA-ARS and University of Florida IFAS — Festival strawberry release notes 2000
University of Florida IFAS Strawberry Research Center, Dover, FL
Florida Strawberry Growers Association variety production statistics
Gulf States Strawberry Industry Association — Variety Performance Guide
